title: Waterfall Model — Software Development Life Cycle
examTip: Waterfall is linear-sequential — each phase must complete before next begins. No going back (no iteration). Customer sees product only at the end. Best for projects with well-defined, stable requirements.

phases.name: 1. Requirements Analysis
phases.description: Gather all system requirements from stakeholders → produce SRS document

phases.name: 2. System Design
phases.description: Translate requirements into system architecture: HLD + LLD

phases.name: 3. Implementation (Coding)
phases.description: Developers write source code based on LLD; unit testing done per module

phases.name: 4. Testing & Integration
phases.description: All modules integrated and tested: integration, system, UAT, performance

phases.name: 5. Deployment
phases.description: Tested software released to production; user training, installation, configuration

phases.name: 6. Maintenance
phases.description: Post-deployment: bug fixes, patches, updates, performance improvements

theory:
The Waterfall Model is the oldest and most structured Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) model, first formalized by Dr. Winston W. Royce in 1970. It follows a strictly linear-sequential approach where each phase must be fully completed and formally reviewed before the next phase begins — like water cascading down a waterfall, it flows only in one direction.

Phase 1 — Requirements Analysis: All functional and non-functional requirements are gathered from stakeholders through interviews, workshops, and document analysis. The output is a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document — the complete baseline that all subsequent phases depend on.

Phase 2 — System Design: Requirements are transformed into a system architecture. High-Level Design (HLD) defines the overall system structure, modules, and their interactions. Low-Level Design (LLD) specifies detailed module-level design, algorithms, data structures, and database schemas.

Phase 3 — Implementation: Developers write source code in the chosen programming language, strictly following the LLD. Each module is independently unit-tested. This is the longest phase.

Phase 4 — Testing & Integration: All developed modules are integrated and tested as a complete system. Testing types include integration testing (module interactions), system testing (end-to-end), user acceptance testing (UAT), and performance testing. Bugs are reported and fixed.

Phase 5 — Deployment: The approved software is deployed to the production environment, which includes installation, configuration, data migration, and user training.

Phase 6 — Maintenance: Ongoing support including corrective maintenance (bug fixes), adaptive maintenance (OS/hardware changes), perfective maintenance (feature enhancements), and preventive maintenance (refactoring).

Advantages: Simple and easy to understand; clearly defined phases; works well for small, well-defined projects; easy to manage due to rigidity.
Disadvantages: Inflexible to changing requirements; customer sees product only at end; high risk for long projects; not suitable for complex or Object-Oriented projects; testing occurs very late.
keyPoints: Linear-sequential model: each phase completes before next begins — no iteration or going back
6 phases: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Output of each phase is input to next: SRS → HLD/LLD → Code → Test Report → Production
Disadvantage: Customer sees final product only at the end; late testing increases risk and cost
Best suited for: short projects with fixed, well-understood, stable requirements
useCases: Government and defense projects with strict contractual requirements and fixed scope
Small embedded system projects where requirements are fully known upfront
Legacy system maintenance where changes are minimal and well-defined
